critique.summary: The article announces the first preview of Avalonia backend support for .NET MAUI, enabling deployment of .NET MAUI apps on Linux and WebAssembly platforms. It highlights the benefits of Avalonia's drawn UI approach for cross-platform consistency and describes testing efforts with existing .NET MAUI apps to validate functionality.
mainPoints: Avalonia backend for .NET MAUI preview allows deployment on Linux and WebAssembly.
Avalonia offers a consistent drawn UI across platforms, contrasting with native UI approaches.
The integration improves Avalonia itself, including new navigation APIs in Avalonia 12.
Testing involved porting existing .NET MAUI apps like MauiPlanets, 2048, AlohaAI, and MyConference.
Avalonia MAUI supports .NET MAUI GraphicsView and SkiaSharp.Views.Maui libraries.
Future plans include expanding Maui.Essentials support and interoperability with WinUI.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with publicly available information from Avalonia and .NET MAUI official sources. Avalonia is known as a cross-platform UI framework that supports drawing its own controls, and recent developments have focused on integrating with .NET MAUI to extend platform support to Linux and WebAssembly. The described apps and testing approaches align with typical open-source development practices. The article is a direct announcement from Avalonia's official blog, lending credibility to the technical claims.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential performance differences between native and drawn UI approaches.
It does not mention any limitations or known issues with the Avalonia backend for .NET MAUI preview.
No comparison is made regarding developer experience or ecosystem maturity between Avalonia MAUI and native .NET MAUI.
The article assumes familiarity with .NET MAUI and Avalonia frameworks without introductory explanations.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the introduction of MAUI support for Linux, with mixed opinions about its relevance and technical challenges. Some commenters express skepticism about Microsoft's commitment to MAUI and compare it to Avalonia, while others highlight technical difficulties with Wayland and pricing concerns.

comments.author: exceptione
comments.text:
From a quick look, I can't find a reason. why? Even MS doesn't fully believe in Maui, as it seems they reblessed WPF. For Avalonia to do the work of MS seems weird, their own free regular WPF-like Avalonia UI toolkit is already the standard for cross desktop development.

I was looking for the line: Microsoft sponsored us, and even then I would not understand why they would spend effort on a doomed project. I know Avalonia being a small company has a big task ahead of porting Avalonia UI to Wayland, which makes porting MS semi-abandonware all the more confusing.

But since these people aren't idiots, I gladly assume I am missing something.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on Microsoft's commitment to MAUI and Avalonia's position, reflecting an informed skepticism that aligns with known industry observations.

comments.author: Ciantic
comments.text:
I wish they support Linux wholeheartedly, a lot of toolkits and GUI frameworks do it by half-assing things, mostly because Wayland is difficult to understand.

In Wayland you have multiple ways to render windows, not just the XDG top level window. It works via surfaces, and here is a list I've discovered so far:

  - XDG Top Level Window
  - Child Window
  - Popup Surface
  - Layer surface (like task-bars, shell overlays)
  - Subsurface (region in another surface)
  - IME Panel Surface (surface that follows text cursor)
There probably is others too.

It is diffifcult to find high-level toolkits that support all of the above.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately highlights the complexity of Wayland and the challenges GUI frameworks face, providing technical insight that is relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: pjmlp
comments.text:
The rewrite from Xamarin.Forms into MAUI, has given a bad taste to many in the community, and kudos to Avalonia to make it happen on GNU/Linux.

By the way on macOS MAUI uses Catalyst as backend, not native macOS APIs.

Also it is kind of interesting that Miguel de Icaza, nowadays completely switched into Swift ecosystem, and is the responsible for making game development on iPad with Godot a reality. Or porting old .NET ideas of his into Swift.
comments.analysis: This comment contextualizes MAUI's community reception and Avalonia's achievements, adding relevant background about key figures, which is mostly accurate.

comments.author: general1465
comments.text:
What is unclear to me, is how does it work with Avalonia pricing wise? If I am having commercial application for Windows, Android, MacOS, iOS (Microsoft MAUI range) then according to [1] I would need to dish out 125000 EUR per application. But it was never clear to me what are the conditions which actually triggers the difference between free and paid plan.

[1] https://avaloniaui.net/xpf/pricing
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about Avalonia's pricing model, though without further details it remains speculative; still a constructive contribution.

comments.author: politelemon
comments.text: I like the possibilities this opens up but I'm struggling to understand how wasm is involved. I had the impression it doesn't have a user interface, but it's called by javascript instead.
comments.analysis: Expresses confusion about WebAssembly's role, which is a common misunderstanding; the comment is honest but lacks technical depth.

comments.author: soumyaskartha
comments.text: Microsoft adding Linux support for yet another framework nobody asked for while WinForms still exists in 2026 is very on brand.
comments.analysis: This comment is a sarcastic critique of Microsoft's product strategy; while it reflects a sentiment shared by some, it is more emotional than constructive.

comments.author: blendergeek
comments.text:
Just a reminder that this MAUI has nothing to do with the pre-existing cross platform UI framework MauiKit from MAUI Project.

https://mauikit.org/
comments.analysis: Provides a helpful clarification to avoid confusion between similarly named projects, adding value to the discussion.

comments.author: tonyedwardspz
comments.text: Excited for this. I do wonder how much effort it will be to get an existing app working with this.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and a practical concern about adoption effort, though it is a brief and general comment.
